finding: The .tp/.gitignore guarantee is scoped to 'when tp first creates the .tp/ directory', but §11.2 explicitly blesses creating .tp/config.json by hand. A hand-created .tp/ has no .gitignore; a later `tp use` or `tp set --local` writes local.json into that pre-existing directory without adding an ignore rule, so per-developer local.json can be committed, violating 2.1.2's MUST NOT.
suggestion: Require tp to ensure .tp/.gitignore (containing local.json) exists whenever it writes local.json, not only when it first creates the .tp/ directory.

finding: Both reference '§5.1' for the active-file pointer / discovery chain and tp use, but after the round-1 renumbering the active-file pointer is §7.1 and layered-resolution reads are §5. §5.1 no longer exists, so these cross-references are dangling. A reader following '§5.1' lands on the wrong (or nonexistent) section.
suggestion: Repoint both '§5.1' references to '§7.1' (Active-file pointer).

finding: Two cross-references still point to §5.1 as the active-file pointer: §3.1 note 3 ('`active` is resolved by the discovery chain in §5.1') and §4.4 ('`active` is written only by `tp use` (§5.1)'). After the round-2 renumbering §5 is now 'Layered resolution is used everywhere workflow fields are read' with no subsections; the active-file discovery chain is §7.1. Both references are stale and send an implementer to the wrong section.
location: 3.1 Precedence (note 3) and 4. Editing the project config (item 4)
suggestion: Repoint both §5.1 references to §7.1 (Active-file pointer).

finding: 'tp config --extract hoists a field only when all discovered task files agree on its value' is undefined when the field is PRESENT in some files and ABSENT in others. Does an absent field count as the built-in default participating in the agreement (so present=8 vs absent=implicit-0 disagree, no hoist), or is it excluded from the agreement test (all present values equal, hoist)? Two implementations produce different hoist sets.
suggestion: Specify how absent fields participate: e.g. hoist only when every discovered file sets the field explicitly and all values are equal; a field absent in any file is not hoisted.

finding: Drift compares each task-file override 'against the project config value' but does not define the baseline when .tp/config.json omits that field yet the task file sets it (present as an override, absent from the project config). It is unstated whether the comparison uses the built-in default or whether an override of a field the project leaves unset counts as drift at all.
suggestion: Specify that a task-file override is compared against the resolved project value (project config field if present, else the built-in default), and state explicitly whether overriding a field the project config leaves at its built-in default is reported.

finding: The recursion skips only `.git`, `.tp`, `node_modules`, and `vendor` directories, but §2.2.2 notes that git worktrees and submodules use a `.git` FILE, not a directory. A `.git` file is not a directory and is not skipped, so the scan descends into nested submodules/worktrees and collects another project's *.tasks.json. Likewise a nested sub-project's own `.tp/` is skipped as a directory but its sibling task files in that subtree are still collected.
suggestion: Stop recursion at any nested repository boundary (`.git` file or directory) and at any subtree owning its own `.tp/`, so the project-wide scan never crosses into a sub-project.

finding: The schema declares `active` (string) and `defaults` (object) but specifies no handling for type-mismatched values: a non-string `active` (number/bool) or a non-boolean `defaults.<flag>` (e.g. compact:"yes"). 2.3.4 covers out-of-range workflow numbers only, leaving local.json value-type errors' read-time behavior undefined.
suggestion: State that a type-mismatched `active` or `defaults` value is rejected with a validation warning and treated as unset (mirroring 7.2.2), so read-time behavior is deterministic.

finding: Write validation for `tp set --local defaults.<flag>=<bool>` is unspecified for unknown flag names and for the intentionally-excluded `json` flag (§8.1). §4.2 gives write-time rejection for out-of-range workflow values, but there is no analogous rule for `defaults` writes, so it is unclear whether `tp set --local defaults.json=…` or `defaults.<unknown>=…` is rejected at write time or silently written and only warned on read.
suggestion: Define whether `tp set --local` rejects (exit 1) unknown or excluded flag keys at write time, or accepts and defers to the read-time unknown-key warning of 2.3.3.

finding: `--extract` 'refuses when .tp/config.json already exists unless --force', but the semantics of --force against an existing config are undefined: does it overwrite the whole file, or merge the newly-hoisted fields into the existing workflow block, and what wins for fields already present there? This gap survives the revision (the earlier merge-vs-overwrite question was only partly addressed by adding the --force gate).
suggestion: Define --force behavior explicitly (overwrite vs field-level merge) and how it reconciles fields already present in the existing .tp/config.json.

finding: '.tp/.gitignore' is written only 'When tp first creates the .tp/ directory'. When .tp/ already exists (e.g. a repo that hand-committed .tp/config.json without a .gitignore), a later 'tp use' creates .tp/local.json but is NOT 'first creating' the directory, so no .gitignore is written and local.json is left committable. The trigger is tied to directory creation, not to local.json creation.
suggestion: Specify that tp ensures .tp/.gitignore (containing local.json) exists whenever it writes .tp/local.json, not only when it first creates the .tp/ directory.

finding: The scan recurses 'into all subdirectories down to the repository boundary' — but the repository boundary (§2.2.2) is an upward ancestor (.git dir OR .git FILE), not a downward limit, so the stopping rule for descent is undefined. 10.2 skips only directory names (.git, .tp, node_modules, vendor); a nested git submodule/worktree uses a .git FILE, which is not skipped, so the submodule's *.tasks.json would still be collected — contradicting Non-goal 2 (no cross-project resolution).
suggestion: Rephrase to 'recurse into descendant subdirectories' and add: stop descending into any directory that contains a nested .git (dir or file), i.e. do not cross into nested repositories/submodules.

finding: §3.1.2 states 'defaults ... is resolved across ranks 1, 2, 4, 5' — asserting a rank-2 (env) layer for every default. Only no_color has a documented env var (NO_COLOR, §8.3). No env var is defined for 'compact' or 'quiet', so their rank-2 layer is unspecified. A tester cannot write 'env overrides compact default' because no env var name exists.
location: 3.1 Precedence (item 2, line 98) and 8. Global flag defaults
suggestion: Either name the env vars for compact/quiet (e.g. TP_COMPACT/TP_QUIET) or state that only no_color has a rank-2 env layer and compact/quiet resolve across ranks 1, 4, 5 only.

finding: A file whose top-level JSON is valid but not an object (e.g. '[]', '42', '"x"') is unhandled: 2.3.5 covers parse failure (malformed), 2.3.3 covers unknown top-level keys (a non-object has no keys), and 2.3.6 covers empty file vs '{}'. A valid non-object JSON value falls through every rule, so two implementers could either treat it as '{}' (no keys) or abort with exit 3.
suggestion: Add a rule: a config whose top-level JSON value is not an object is malformed and handled by 2.3.5 (exit 3).

finding: Drift reports each override that 'differs from the project config value', but the behavior when .tp/config.json is present yet OMITS that field is unspecified. With no project value to compare against, it is undefined whether a task-file override is a deviation (compared against the built-in default) or not a deviation (nothing to deviate from). 9.4 only covers the entirely-absent-config case.
suggestion: Define the comparison baseline when the project config omits a field: e.g. compare against the resolved project value (project field if present, else built-in default) and flag any override that differs.

finding: The --resolved `source` enum is cli|env|override|project|default, but the example annotates `active` (whose origin per §7.1.1 may be local.json, legacy .tp-active, or auto-detect) and `defaults` (origin local.json) with source "project". local.json is labeled 'project-local' in the rank-4 row, not 'project', and legacy .tp-active / auto-detect have no source value at all. The provenance vocabulary for `active`/`defaults` is undefined and the example uses a value that conflates the project config with the project-local file.
suggestion: Extend the enum (e.g. add local/legacy/autodetect) and state which source vocabulary applies to `active` and `defaults` versus workflow fields; correct the example so a local.json origin is not labeled 'project'.

finding: The prose says '--resolved annotates each WORKFLOW field with a value and the source layer', but the example also annotates 'active' and 'defaults.compact' with {value,source}. The source enum is cli|env|override|project|default, yet per §3.1 item 2 workflow fields have no CLI or env layer, so 'cli' and 'env' are unreachable for workflow fields (they are only reachable for defaults/active). Whether active/defaults are annotated, and which sources are valid per field type, is contradictory between prose and example.
suggestion: State that --resolved annotates workflow, active, and defaults fields; and clarify that cli/env sources apply only to defaults (and active/--file), never to workflow fields.

finding: The source enum is `cli|env|override|project|default`, with no value for the git-ignored `.tp/local.json` layer, yet §3.1 rank 4 deliberately splits into 'project' (.tp/config.json) and 'project-local' (.tp/local.json). The section's own example labels `active` and `defaults.compact` — which can only come from local.json — as source:'project', collapsing committed policy and per-developer local state into one label and defeating the two-file split that is this release's headline. `active`'s provenance is also a discovery-chain rank (§7.1: --file/TP_FILE/local.json/legacy/auto-detect), none of which map to these five sources. The prose further says it 'annotates each workflow field' while the example annotates the non-workflow keys `active` and `defaults`.
suggestion: Add a distinct source value (e.g. 'local'/'project-local') for `.tp/local.json`-sourced values, relabel the example's `active`/`defaults` accordingly, and define the source vocabulary for `active` (discovery-chain ranks) and for the non-workflow keys the prose currently excludes.

finding: §3.1.3 says 'active is resolved by the discovery chain in §5.1', but the round-2 reorg moved the active-file pointer out of §5 into §7 — §5 is now 'Layered resolution is used everywhere workflow fields are read' and has no §5.1. The active-file discovery chain now lives in §7.1.1. The cross-reference is stale and misdirects the reader.
suggestion: Change '§5.1' to '§7.1' (Active-file pointer).

finding: §4.4 says 'active is written only by tp use (§5.1)', but §5.1 no longer exists after the round-2 renumbering that moved the active-file pointer to §7. tp use writing the active key is now defined in §7.1.2. This reference was correct when active was §5.1 in round 1 and was not updated during the reorg.
suggestion: Change '§5.1' to '§7.1' (Active-file pointer).

finding: '`defaults` … is resolved across ranks 1, 2, 4, 5' asserts a rank-2 environment layer for flag defaults, but only `no_color` has a defined env var (NO_COLOR, §8.3); no env var backs `compact` or `quiet`. The general 'not every field passes through every rank' caveat softens but does not resolve which env var, if any, applies to compact/quiet.
suggestion: Clarify that the rank-2 env layer for defaults applies only to no_color (via NO_COLOR) and that compact/quiet have no env layer, or name their env vars if they exist.

finding: The prose states --resolved 'annotates each workflow field with a value and the source layer', but the accompanying example also annotates `active` and `defaults`, which are not workflow fields. The set of fields --resolved annotates is described more narrowly than the example demonstrates.
suggestion: Reword to 'annotates each field' (workflow, active, and defaults) or scope both the prose and the example to the same field set.

finding: §11.4 gives exit 4 for --extract refusing on a dirty git tree, but assigns no exit code to the other refusal it defines: 'refuses when .tp/config.json already exists unless --force'. The exit code for the already-exists refusal is unspecified, so a CI check for that condition is nondeterministic.
suggestion: State the exit code for the 'config already exists without --force' refusal (e.g. exit 4).

finding: '--extract refuses to run on a dirty git working tree (exit 4)' assumes a git repository. Behavior outside a git repo (no working tree to check) is unspecified: does --extract skip the dirty check and run, or refuse? Combined with §10's scan recursing to the filesystem root when there is no repository boundary, running --extract outside a repo is undefined.
suggestion: Specify --extract behavior when not inside a git repository (e.g. refuse with a hint, since the dirty-tree safety check cannot be performed).

finding: §4.1 specifies creating `.tp/`, `.tp/.gitignore`, and `.tp/config.json` when absent for `tp set --workflow --project`, and §7.1.2 does so for `tp use`, but §4.4's `tp set --local defaults.<flag>=<bool>` write path never states whether it creates `.tp/`, `.tp/.gitignore`, and `.tp/local.json` when none exist. The creation semantics of the --local write are undefined.
suggestion: State that `tp set --local` creates `.tp/`, `.tp/.gitignore`, and `.tp/local.json` at the repository root when absent, mirroring §4.1/§7.1.2.

finding: 4.1 locates the created .tp/ at 'the repository root — the directory containing .git', but §2.2.2's boundary explicitly recognizes a `.git` FILE (worktrees/submodules) as well as a `.git` directory. 4.1 omits the file case, leaving the creation location ambiguous inside a git worktree.
suggestion: Make 4.1 reference the same boundary definition as 2.2.2 ('the first ancestor containing a .git directory or .git file').

finding: 2.3.3 says unknown keys are 'reported as a validation warning on stderr' without naming any command, while 2.3.4 (out-of-range) explicitly limits its warning to 'tp validate/tp config'. It is inconsistent whether the unknown-key warning fires from every config-consuming command (e.g. tp plan) or only from tp validate/tp config; a tester cannot assert which commands warn.
location: 2.3 Schema and validation (item 3 vs item 4, lines 67-72)
suggestion: Align 2.3.3 with 2.3.4: name the commands that emit the unknown-key warning (presumably tp validate/tp config).

finding: Note 2 states `defaults` is 'resolved across ranks 1, 2, 4, 5 only', but only `no_color` has a rank-2 environment peer (NO_COLOR, per §8.3); `compact` and `quiet` have no environment variable and never pass through rank 2. The explicit rank list over-generalizes and conflicts with §8, which defines an env layer for no_color alone.
suggestion: Reword to 'ranks 1, 4, 5 (plus rank 2 for no_color via NO_COLOR)', or drop the specific rank list and rely on the general caveat that not every field passes through every rank.

finding: §6.2's --resolved source enum is one of {cli, env, override, project, default} and the example labels values sourced from .tp/local.json (active, defaults.compact) as source 'project'. But §3.1's rank-4 note distinguishes '.tp/config.json workflow (project)' from '.tp/local.json defaults (project-local)'. The single 'project' token conflates committed policy (config.json) with git-ignored local state (local.json), undercutting the two-file split this round introduced; --resolved cannot show whether a value came from the committed or the local file.
suggestion: Either add a distinct 'project-local' (or 'local') source token to the §6.2 enum for local.json-sourced values (active, defaults.*) and use it in the example, or align §3.1's rank-4 terminology to a single 'project' label for both files.
